source_paper_title: The Complexity of Recognizing Facets for the Knapsack Polytope
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Chen_2025, title={The Complexity of Recognizing Facets for the Knapsack Polytope}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0481}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0481}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Chen, Rui and Zhu, Haoran}, year={2025}, month=nov }

Section 5 (Concluding Remarks), page 16 (in the provided pagination: page 16/17 of the parsed text).

It is still open whether it can be done in time \(f(K)\cdot \mathrm{poly}(n)\) where \(f\) is some computable function independent of \(n\), i.e., whether the knapsack facet recognition problem is fixed-parameter tractable.
title: Fixed-parameter tractable facet recognition for knapsack polytopes by coefficient types
background_latex:
A (0--1) knapsack set is
\[Q:=\{x\in\{0,1\}^n: a^\top x\le b\},\]
where \(a\in\mathbb{N}^n\) and \(b\in\mathbb{N}\). The associated knapsack polytope is the convex hull
\[P:=\mathrm{conv}(Q)\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n.\]
Consider an integer linear inequality
\[\alpha^\top x\le \beta,\]
with \((\alpha,\beta)\in\mathbb{Z}^{n+1}_+\) (nonnegative integer coefficients). Such an inequality is called valid for \(P\) if it holds for all \(x\in Q\) (equivalently, for all \(x\in P\)). A valid inequality defines a facet of \(P\) if the face
\[F:=\{x\in P: \alpha^\top x=\beta\}
\]
has dimension \(\dim(P)-1\) (equivalently, \(F\) contains \(\dim(P)\) affinely independent points of \(P\)).

Define the parameter
\[K:=|\alpha|_+ := \left|\{\alpha_i: \alpha_i>0,\ i\in[n]\}\right|,\]
the number of distinct positive values taken by the coefficients of \(\alpha\). (Coefficients equal to \(0\) are not counted.)

The decision problem KNAPSACK FACETS asks: given \((a,b)\) and \((\alpha,\beta)\), decide whether \(\alpha^\top x\le \beta\) defines a facet of \(P\). The paper shows that KNAPSACK FACETS is \(\mathrm{D}^p\)-complete in general, and also gives an algorithm running in time \(n^{K+O(1)}\) (an XP algorithm) parameterized by \(K\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether KNAPSACK FACETS parameterized by \(K=|\alpha|_+\) is fixed-parameter tractable: does there exist a computable function \(f:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}\), independent of \(n\), such that for every instance consisting of \(a\in\mathbb{N}^n\), \(b\in\mathbb{N}\), and \((\alpha,\beta)\in\mathbb{Z}^{n+1}_+\) with \(K=|\alpha|_+\), one can decide whether \(\alpha^\top x\le\beta\) defines a facet of \(P=\mathrm{conv}(\{x\in\{0,1\}^n: a^\top x\le b\})\) in time
\[f(K)\cdot \mathrm{poly}(n)?\]
latex:
A (0--1) knapsack set is
\[Q:=\{x\in\{0,1\}^n: a^\top x\le b\},\]
where \(a\in\mathbb{N}^n\) and \(b\in\mathbb{N}\). The associated knapsack polytope is the convex hull
\[P:=\mathrm{conv}(Q)\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n.\]
Consider an integer linear inequality
\[\alpha^\top x\le \beta,\]
with \((\alpha,\beta)\in\mathbb{Z}^{n+1}_+\) (nonnegative integer coefficients). Such an inequality is called valid for \(P\) if it holds for all \(x\in Q\) (equivalently, for all \(x\in P\)). A valid inequality defines a facet of \(P\) if the face
\[F:=\{x\in P: \alpha^\top x=\beta\}
\]
has dimension \(\dim(P)-1\) (equivalently, \(F\) contains \(\dim(P)\) affinely independent points of \(P\)).

Define the parameter
\[K:=|\alpha|_+ := \left|\{\alpha_i: \alpha_i>0,\ i\in[n]\}\right|,\]
the number of distinct positive values taken by the coefficients of \(\alpha\). (Coefficients equal to \(0\) are not counted.)

The decision problem KNAPSACK FACETS asks: given \((a,b)\) and \((\alpha,\beta)\), decide whether \(\alpha^\top x\le \beta\) defines a facet of \(P\). The paper shows that KNAPSACK FACETS is \(\mathrm{D}^p\)-complete in general, and also gives an algorithm running in time \(n^{K+O(1)}\) (an XP algorithm) parameterized by \(K\).

Determine whether KNAPSACK FACETS parameterized by \(K=|\alpha|_+\) is fixed-parameter tractable: does there exist a computable function \(f:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}\), independent of \(n\), such that for every instance consisting of \(a\in\mathbb{N}^n\), \(b\in\mathbb{N}\), and \((\alpha,\beta)\in\mathbb{Z}^{n+1}_+\) with \(K=|\alpha|_+\), one can decide whether \(\alpha^\top x\le\beta\) defines a facet of \(P=\mathrm{conv}(\{x\in\{0,1\}^n: a^\top x\le b\})\) in time
\[f(K)\cdot \mathrm{poly}(n)?\]

open_problem_quote: It is still open whether it can be done in time \(f(K)\cdot \mathrm{poly}(n)\) where \(f\) is some computable function independent of \(n\), i.e., whether the knapsack facet recognition problem is fixed-parameter tractable.
subject_classification: Parameterized complexity of polyhedral decision problems
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The problem asks for existence of an FPT algorithm rather than a universally quantified inequality; automated search is unlikely to refute FPT without major complexity assumptions. While one could attempt to find lower-bound evidence via parameterized reductions, this is not a finite instance search task.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate algorithms can be encoded and benchmarked on generated instances with parameter \(K\), but conclusively establishing an \(f(K)\cdot\mathrm{poly}(n)\) guarantee requires proofs beyond empirical evaluation. Automated experimentation could still help discover algorithmic ideas or special-case tractability.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
